← Back to Blog

MCS

📅 March 08, 2023

By the time this particular post is published, the whole sorry episode of my time at Malvern College Switzerland will be well and truly in the rearview mirror. But I'm writing contemporaneously - just a few days after I was told my services were no longer required - to ensure that what I relate is as accurate as possible.

Some will see sour grapes in this. But my conscience is clear: I was badly treated by the powers that be and I want my experience to act as a warning to others.

It all started back in December 2020 when, after a covid-induced change to my plans to cycle back from Thailand to the UK, I was offered the position of Founding Headmaster of Malvern College Switzerland. Perhaps alarm bells should have gone off back then when, having secured the position, my contract arrived with my name misspelt and the offer coming from 'Feydey International' with no reference to Malvern UK the sponsoring brand who's name and reputation attracted me in the first place.

I was due to start on January 15th 2021, initially part-time, then full-time from April with the school due to open in September 2021. I remember at the time thinking that this was a tight schedule, but was reassured that the owner had a network that could fill such a school in just 9 months. I got to work - creating videos, writing copy for the website, planning the curriculum and staffing requirements etc. It was busy.

Most of my dealings were with the owner, but there was a small team of others working with him in Switzerland who I would also occasionally converse with. A few irritations began to emerge, but nothing that wouldn't have been expected from an organisation in its infancy.

One big concern, though, was the website which to my mind was house-on-fire urgent to get on with, finish and publish to the world. This was entirely in the control of the owner who would edit copy to make it unintelligible, endlessly tinker with the design and keep changing his mind over what it was we were offering. He blamed Malvern UK for the hold-ups, but I could see that the problem lay with him. Revisions were endless and typos inveigled themselves everywhere as copy sent to him was duly 'improved'. Meanwhile, the clock ticked onwards.

Sensing that I was better leaving the marketing side to the owner, and buoyed by him telling me that he'd 'fill the school if I ran it', I set to the task of recruiting teachers. Malvern UK had helped me convince the owner that we needed to get on with this soon and that it was best done through the TES Gold package. I drew up the applicant pack, the adverts and the various job descriptions. Before long we had six excited and very well-qualified teachers signed up.

Midway through the recruitment process my first month of employment came and went. Nothing was mentioned about pay and nothing materialised, but I wasn't over-concerned. After all, this was a start-up and systems were not yet in place. I decided to wait a while and see how things developed. 

During the second month, I started to become privately concerned about the lack of progress with the website and the complacency about pupil recruitment. We had no pupils signed up and no one tasked specifically with resolving this problem. I had been told on appointment that we would soon be joined by a full-time marketing and admissions expert, but this never happened. Instead, recruitment was being overseen by the owner aided intermittently by an enthusiastic but inexperienced accomplice who was helping in his spare time. Much was being promised, but nothing seemed to be happening.

Recognising the need for some coordination and direction, I asked for a meeting with the Swiss team so that we could formulate a plan of action. It was a delicate situation politically because I hadn't yet set foot in the school. The owner was managing the team in Switzerland but this team, it transpired, were not in his employ but were working for the holiday camp outfit that owned the building. My request for a meeting with the team was declined. The message was, in so many words, 'get back in your box'.

Chastened, I decided to fall back on the things where I could make progress - policies, handbooks, joining instructions and staff planning. Meanwhile the owner was churning out rather amatauer looking Facebook ads at great expense. The services of an online marketing expert were called in but I witnessed relations turning sour when payments were missed. and his various business partners over pay or broken agreements. At this stage though I was determined to give him the benefit of the doubt. Surely Malvern wouldn't lend it's name to a school where there was doubts about probity, I reasoned to myself.

By mid-March alarm bells really started to ring. When, at the end of my second month without pay I raised the issue politely at the end of one of a call with the owner I was met with an angry response calibrated to make me feel guilty for asking for what had been agreed. The irony here is that I would have been happy to work on a pro bono basis for a while had it been clear from the outset that this was the arrangement. I had been planning a sabbatical in any case and was in the fortunate position of being financially secure. However this wasn't the deal. I began to feel that I was being taken for a ride.

That fractious phone call ended with no resolution and so I contacted the team at MCUK. They were sympathetic, but it was made clear to me that this was my battle to fight, not theirs. Faced with a choice - walk away and get nothing or approach the problem in a conciliatory way and leave the door open to recouping some of what I was owed I went for the former. It's important to stress here that this was only two months in and I had done absolutely everything that could have been asked of me at this stage and - even though I say so myself - had done it well.

I called the owner and said that I wasn't prepared to work for free, because that wasn't what we had agreed, but that if he had cash flow problems I was prepared to accept salary payments on a two-monthly basis instead. I would need paying now, of course, two months in, if I was to continue any further. Within a day or so an amount of money turned up in my account that bore no relation to what had been agreed. I was significantly out of pocket. I asked several times over the next few months for a break down of the calculations to arrive at that figure but none was forthcoming and so I let it lie.

I was in regular communication with MCUK at this stage and together we agreed that to dwell on this and make a fuss would only end with a Pyrrhich victory. We had a school to open and I was determined to show that I had thick skin and hardy disposition that had attracted me to a startup in the first place.

We then moved into April and slowly a few pupils signed ip

Jo ignored

Moddy with Americans

WhatsApp

Too many options

Sixth Form plan

Mack
